title: How to Get Grass Stains Off Golf Shoes
description: Grass stains set fast on white golf shoes. Here's the routine that lifts them without wrecking the mesh or upper underneath.

body.text: You finish eighteen holes and notice it on the walk to the car: a green smear across the toe box, right where you followed through on a wedge shot from the rough. Grass stains are one of the fastest, most stubborn marks a round of golf leaves behind — and the longer they sit, the harder they are to lift.
Why grass stains set so fast

Grass stains aren't dirt sitting on the surface — they're chlorophyll and plant juice that bond to the fibers of the material, especially on lighter leather and mesh. Heat speeds that bond up, so a stain that sits in a hot trunk overnight is noticeably harder to remove than one treated the same day.

items: Work the stain while it's still fresh — don't let it dry and set overnight
Wet the area lightly first; a stain that's damp responds better than one that's bone dry or soaked through
Brush in one direction with light, even pressure instead of scrubbing back and forth, which can push the stain deeper into the material
Wipe clean with a separate towel so you're lifting the stain away, not just spreading it around
Let shoes air dry away from direct heat — a vent, radiator, or car dashboard can set whatever stain is left behind

body.text: Skip bleach and harsh household cleaners on golf shoes — they can discolor leather and break down the glue on synthetic uppers. And resist the urge to scrub hard on a fresh stain; aggressive pressure on wet grass residue tends to grind it further into the material instead of lifting it out.

G-SCRUB's foaming cleaner and brush are built for exactly this routine — a concentrated, direct-to-brush formula that helps remove grass stains and course debris, safe for most common golf shoe materials when used as directed.
